converted from EAD 1.0 to EAD 2002 >>>>>>> 2e3ec5c57314077b8435567632a49d313357daab
English
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Samuel A. Elliot collection
Elliot, Samuel A.
Huntington Free Library
9165
1.2 cubic feet. 1.2 cubic feet.
1927-1935. Legal documents, manuscripts. Contains reports to the Board of Indian commissioners, reports 1-107, bulletins regarding Indians to the 70th Congress, and bulletins from the American Indian Defense Association, Inc. The items are typed mimeos. Indexes for the 70th Congress and American Indian Defense association papers and for the Indian Commissioners reports are included. Various authors. A wide variety of topics are covered including Indian and government relations, land tenure, living conditions, laws, and government programs. Focus on western United States Indian communities, reservations, and tribes including, Pueblo, Apache, Navajo, Salish, Cherokee, and Dakota. Agency reports from Sac and Fox, Winnebago, Pima, Consolidated Chippewa, Rocky Boy, St. Regis, Yakima, Western Shoshone, and San Carlos Indian agencies and reservations. Authors include Flora Warren Seymour, Hugh L. Scott, Earl Y. Henderson, Mary Vaux Walcott, Clement S. Ucker and John J. Sullivan. Collection material in English <<<<<<< HEAD 46 - folder 2 46 - folder 3 46 - folder 4 46 - folder 5 46 - folder 6 46 - folder 7 46 - folder 8 46 - folder 9 46 - folder 10 46 - folder 11 46 - folder 12 46 - folder 1 ======= 46 - folder 2 46 - folder 3 46 - folder 4 46 - folder 5 46 - folder 6 46 - folder 7 46 - folder 8 46 - folder 9 46 - folder 10 46 - folder 11 46 - folder 12 46 - folder 1 >>>>>>> 2e3ec5c57314077b8435567632a49d313357daab 5734386
General
Contact Information:
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu
Compiled by:
Evan Fay Earle
Date completed:
February 2006
EAD encoding:
Evan Fay Earle, February 2006
COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
Contains reports to the Board of Indian commissioners, reports 1-107, bulletins regarding Indians to the 70th Congress, and bulletins from the American Indian Defense Association, Inc. The items are typed mimeos. Indexes for the 70th Congress and American Indian Defense association papers and for the Indian Commissioners reports are included. Various authors. A wide variety of topics are covered including Indian and government relations, land tenure, living conditions, laws, and government programs. Focus on western United States Indian communities, reservations, and tribes including, Pueblo, Apache, Navajo, Salish, Cherokee, and Dakota. Agency reports from Sac and Fox, Winnebago, Pima, Consolidated Chippewa, Rocky Boy, St. Regis, Yakima, Western Shoshone, and San Carlos Indian agencies and reservations. Authors include Flora Warren Seymour, Hugh L. Scott, Earl Y. Henderson, Mary Vaux Walcott, Clement S. Ucker and John J. Sullivan.
COLLECTION ARRANGEMENT
Original Huntington Free Library box numbers have been retained. Box contains multiple unrelated collections. Folder numbers pertain to this specific collection only and are not folder order for the entire box.
SERIES LIST
Series I. Board of Indian Commissioners reports Boxes 5A-5E
Series II. 70th Congress bulletins Box 46, Folders 9165-1 - 9165-3
Series III. American Indian Defense Association, Inc. papers Box 46, Folders 9165-4 - 9165-12
Cite As:
Samuel A. Elliot collection, #9165. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
Yakama Indian Reservation (Wash.). San Carlos Indian Reservation (Ariz.). Saint Regis Mohawk Indian Reservation (N.Y.). Sac and Fox Reservation (Iowa). Rocky Boy\'s Reservation (Mont.). Skitswish Indians. Seminole Indians. Salish Indians. Pueblo Indians. Pima Indians. Navajo Indians. Dakota Indians. Cherokee Indians. Apache Indians. Indian reservations. Indians of North America -- West (U.S.). Indians of North America -- Social conditions. Indians of North America -- Land tenure. Indians of North America -- Government relations. Indians of North America -- Education. Indians of North America. Henderson, Earl Y. Scott, Hugh Lenox, 1853-1934. Seymour, Flora Warren, 1888-1948. Sullivan, John J. Ucker, Clement S. Walcott, Mary Vaux, 1860-1940. American Indian Defense Association United States. Board of Indian Commissioners United States. Congress (70th, 1st session ) (Date of meeting or treaty signing: 1928-1929)..) United States. Congress (70th, 2nd session ) (Date of meeting or treaty signing: 1928-1929)..) United States. Office of Indian Affairs. Consolidated Chippewa Agency United States. Office of Indian Affairs. Umatilla Agency United States. Office of Indian Affairs. Western Shoshone Agency United States. Office of Indian Affairs. Winnebago Agency

Report on the Osage Indian Agency, Oklahoma.[undated]5ESeries II. 70th Congress bulletinsGuide469165-1No. 5. The state and the Indians.December 22, 1927469165-2No. 6. "Orgies of Indian Extravagance, wild and fantastic."December 23, 1927469165-2No. 8. Need for immediate action on the California court of claims H.R. 491.December 31. 1927469165-2No. 10. The Indian death rate still rising-what the new census tables disclose.January 9, 1928469165-2No. 11. Indian Bureau Expenditures - States of Montana and North Dakota.January 10, 1928469165-2No. 12. The Flathead water-power site amendment to the pending appropriation bill.January 12, 1928469165-2No. 13. Defeat this bureau scheme for confiscating Indian powers.January 12, 1928469165-2No. 14. What is meant by "commercial value of a power site"?January 12, 1928469165-2No. 15. State cooperation in Indian welfare-some misunderstandings corrected.January 23, 1928469165-2No. 16. Shall the Indian bureau continue to block the development of the Flathead power site?January 23 1928469165-2No. 17. A new assault against Indian water-power rights, The Carlos Apaches are the victims in the pending appropriation bill.January 25, 1928469165-2No. 18. Value of the Flathead power sites.January 27, 1928469165-2No. 20. An Indian tribal governor made spokesman for the Indian Bureau.January 31, 1928469165-2No. 21. Confiscating the Apache water-power site-will the senate commit this action?February 1, 1928469165-2No. 22. The renewed attack against Indian ownership of executive order reservations.February 2, 1928469165-2No. 23. The attack against the Carlos Apache ...Interior Department Bill.February 3, 1928469165-2No. 9-A The proof that Indian welfare effort succeeds and the justification for a new appeal for aid.February 20 1928469165-3To the press of New Mexico: position of the American Indian Defense Association on the middle Rio Conservancy Bill.February 25, 1928469165-3The Rio Grande Conservancy Bill (Pueblo) correction of a mis-statement and some important new facts.February 27, 1928469165-3No. 25. Now the Senate knows the issues in the Conservancy Bill, S.700.February 28, 1928469165-3Arabian Nights argument for the Pueblo Conservancy Bill S.700: total errors statement regarding water priorities and taxation.February 29, 1928469165-3No. 26. The president's veto has been asked for the Pueblo Conservancy Bill.March 6, 1928469165-3No. 27. Fifty Jackson Barnett cases coming safe from court accounting (HR 7204).May 10, 1928469165-3re Pueblo Appropriation in Deficiency Bill.May 21, 1928469165-3The crisis of the Pueblo Indians explanatory statement accompanying copies of documents by Mr. Louis Marshall, attorney for the Pueblo tribes.May 22, 1928469165-3The Indian Bureau's Conservancy scheme is finally exposed.December 21, 1928469165-3Series III. American Indian Defense Association, Inc. papersGuide469165-4Letter to Pueblo tribal officials.November 22, 1927469165-5Written statement to the "Oakland Forum".December 14, 1927469165-5Secretary Work confirms the "Irresponsible Propaganda" and light starts to break on Indian affairs.December 16, 1927469165-5Letter to the members Congress.December 20, 1927469165-5The hidden crisis in forestry situation.January 4, 1928469165-5Letter to Senator Smoot.January 19, 1928469165-5Letter to the officers of the New Mexico Pueblos.January 21, 1928469165-5Letter to members of U.S. Senate in regard to pending vote on Flathead power-site (2 copies).February 2, 1928469165-5The money stake of the Indians in the Flathead Power-site lease controversy.February 6, 1928469165-5"Recall this bill and protect Pueblo Indians"! (Senate Bill 700).February 8, 1928469165-5To the members of the Senate "Broken faith with the Indians and a trick upon Congress", the Pueblo Conservancy Bill (S.700).February 9, 1928469165-5Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy.February 11, 1928469165-5To Senators: the Pueblo outrage which jeopardizes all Indians.February 12, 1928469165-5Rio Grande Conservancy Bill sent back to committee.February 13, 1928469165-5Does the language divert the Flathead Indian revenues?February 14, 1928469165-5To the members of the House and Senate: the Cramton Pueblo Conservancy outrage.February 16, 1928469165-5To the officials of the Pueblos: the Conservancy Bill.February 17, 1928469165-5Statement by John Collier and A. A. Grorud, dealing with certain statements by representative Louis C. Cramton, made on the floor of the House, February 16, 1928.February 17, 1928469165-5To the members of congress: the acknowledged facts which condemn the pending Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy Bill.February 19, 1928469165-5To the several newspapers associations: Middle Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy Bill.February 22, 1928469165-5Mary Austin declares amended Pueblo Conservancy Bill a menace to the Indians and to the Spanish-American white farmers of New MexicoFebruary 23, 1928469165-5A final plea to the Senate on behalf of the Pueblo Indians and of simple national honor.February 24, 1928469165-5"Has the Pueblo irrigated acreage been shrinking within historic times"?February 29, 1928469165-5Bulletin No. 1 of series "Conservancy and reclamation on Indian Lands"; "The nightmare of Indian Bureau reclamation and Conservancy,".January 19, 1928469165-6Fictions and mysteries of the Indian Bureau. Presented by the Bureau of Officers to the Committees of Congress. Haskell Institute Farm Mystery.January 21, 1929469165-6Summary report on the Crow Reservation Irrigation scandal.January 22, 1929469165-6Part of the Indian office record before Congress with respect to the irrigation project on the Crow Reservation.January 22, 1929469165-6Bulletin No. 2 of series, "Conservancy and reclamation of Indian lands", The Fort Belknap, Montana irrigation fiasco and the Indian deceit with respect to it.January 23, 1929469165-6Bulletin No. 3 of series "Conservancy and reclamation of Indian lands", The Fort Peck irrigation collapse.January 1929469165-6Indian Bureau misrepresentations to Congress with respect to its expenditures on the Crow Reservation.January 26, 1929469165-6To the members of the House of Representatives.January 29, 1929469165-6Do Indian children slowly starve?.January 30, 1929469165-6Fast and loose goes the Ute Indian money.January 30, 1929469165-6The Indian Bureau's renewed battle against reform and its whitewash venture of February 1, 1929.February 21929469165-6Apache Indians and the Senate investigation.February 51929469165-6The Indian Bureau's thirty one million dollar joy ride in the guise of reclamation enterprises.February 7, 1929469165-6Governor New Mexico committee ...in the matter of Indian boarding schools.February 9, 1929469165-6Indian boarding schools and the Santa Fe New Mexican.February 11, 1929469165-6Three propaganda statements about Indian boarding schools.February 16, 1929469165-6A statistician to the rescue.February 20, 1929469165-6The Fort Mojave school record and the Bureau's new propaganda.February 28, 1929469165-6Suggestions concerning future Indian policy, legislation, and administration.April 22, 1929469165-6The meeting of the council of all the New Mexico Pueblos.September 2, 1929469165-6Items of the Indian Defense program-Winter of 1929-1930.November 1929469165-6The immediate tasks of the American Indian Defense Association, Inc..December 10, 1929469165-6The Congressman Cramton mystery a partial explanation of his Flathead power site conduct.February 20, 1929469165-7Continued hunger and distress for Indian boarding school children.February 26, 1930469165-7Dr. Haven Emerson asks President Hoover to renew his plea for food and clothing for Indian children.February 28, 1930469165-7The war department set up of Flathead power rentals.February 28, 1930469165-7Hoover's emergency relief request for Indian children and what happened to it?March 4, 1930469165-7Shall the Indian children's hopes, wakened by President Hoover, be dashed.March 6, 1930469165-7Petition to U.S. Senate to amend the deficiency bill.March 10, 1930469165-7The effect of the appropriations just recommended by the Senate Appropriations Committee.March 11, 1930469165-7The Interior Department's construction of the middle Rio Grande Conservancy contract is right.March 14, 1930469165-7The Senate should reconsider its acceptance of the conferee's report on the Indian food and items in the deficiency bill March 18, 1930.March 18, 1930469165-7To the officers of the Pueblos: the Swing-Johnson Indian.March 22, 1930469165-7Exactly what the Hoover food and standard for Indian boarding school children requires in the 1931 Interior Appropriation.March 22, 1930469165-7The record and position of the Indian Bureau, showing that the Indian children must rely on the Senate and their unofficial friends.March 22, 1930469165-7Mr. Walter H. Wheeler's charge against assistant Commissioner Scattergood of the Indian Bureau.March 24, 1930469165-7Part of the testimony of John Collier on the Flathead power site controversy.March 25, 1930469165-7Investigating Indian irrigation and reimbursable indebtedness.March 27, 1930469165-7The Flathead rental set up of the War Department, and its fictitious data obtained from what mysterious agency?March 27, 1930469165-7The Flathead jugglery of the War Department, and its fictitious data obtained from some other agency.March 28, 1930469165-7To the officers of the middle Rio Grande Pueblos: concerning how much money should be paid by the government to Conservancy District.March 31, 1930469165-7The power site decision at verge of injury to the and the Indians.April 4, 1930469165-7The new power proposal of the Department of the Interior.April 10, 1930469165-7Report on the food and clothing appropriation for Indian children.April 10, 1930469165-7Worse and worse grow the revealed intentions of the government departments with respect to the Flathead power site.April 11, 1930469165-7Luxuries for the Indian! "Slow starvation."April 15, 1930469165-7Compares Flathead power site deal to Albert B. Fall record.April 21, 1930469165-7Subsidizing the Anaconda Copper Company in Montana.April 22, 1930469165-7Defeat this Indian Bureau Bill establishing tyranny over the Indians!April 25, 1930469165-7A new espionage over Indians proposed by the new Indian commissioners.April 29, 1930469165-7Copy of the letter addressed by the Board of Directors of the American Indian Defense Association, Inc., to Secretary Ray Lyman Wilbur of the Department of the Interior.May 6, 1930469165-7Editorial page 310, The New Republic, May 7, 1930. "Flogging of Indian children in government boarding schools."May 7, 1930469165-7I. Secretary Wilbur's reply to the Board of Director's letter.May 7, 1930469165-7II. A letter by the Executive Secretary to the President of the Association dealing with Secretary Wilbur's letter.May 7, 1930469165-7I. A letter by Dr. Haven Emerson to Secretary Wilbur.May 7, 1930469165-7II. A letter by the Board of Directors to Secretary Wilbur.May 9, 1930469165-7Cramton and the power trust steal a march on Congress.May 9, 1930469165-7The Flathead license outrage and the Interior Department's ironical defense.May 20, 1930469165-7The Flathead license subverts the federal water power act and destroys public regulation.May 31, 1930469165-7Letter to the officers of the New Mexico Pueblos concerning Bill H.R. 12615.June 3, 1930469165-7The betrayal of regulation through the Flathead power site license.June 4, 1930469165-7Declares Simms Bill a threat to Pueblo Indian life.June 8, 1930469165-7Letter from Secretary of the Interior Wilbur in regards to Flathead Power site.June 12, 1930469165-7The birthright of regulation sold, and the mess of pottage not secured in exchange by the unprecedented Flathead power license.June 18, 1930469165-7The Indian Bureau when defying parental wishes and forcing Indian children into boarding schools.Undated 1931469165-8Senator Frazier, Committee Chairman, moves to protect Pueblo Indians and white settlers of New Mexico (2 copies).January 24, 1931469165-8Representative passes out March 2nd. Thereafter, the Swing-Johnson Bill can certainly be enacted.January 28, 1931469165-8The case against Herbert J. Hagerman of the Indian Bureau as established to date.February 5, 1931469165-8Indian education after two years, the situation demands action, not delay through a $100,000 self-investigation by the delinquent Indian Bureau.February 25, 1931469165-8The Indian Bureau charged with deception or with being the victim of deception in the matter of Navajo land adjustments.February 25, 1931469165-8Dr. Hoffman insists: "All that can reasonably be done is being done to promote the of our Indians." Is it true? A statistician's three horned dilemma.November 10, 1931469165-8Report on El Capitan Grande Indian and the Barona Ranch situation.November 14, 1931469165-8Report on El Capitan Grande Indian and the Barona Ranch situation.November 14, 1931469165-9Destruction of Indian rights and protections by the new grazing permit regulations of the Indian Bureau.January 22, 1932469165-9Improper guardianship.February 3, 1932469165-9Illegal and coercive leasing of Indian lands to whites through the Indian Bureau's permit scheme.February 5, 1932469165-9Letter to the officers of the Pueblos concerning the Compensation Bill.February 6, 1932469165-9Nothing but instantaneous action can save the economic life of the Navajos.February 9, 1932469165-9Can anything force the Indian Bureau to adequate action in the Navajo relief matter?February 12, 1932469165-9Letter to all friends of the Navajo Indians concerning procrastination of Indian Office in responding to sheep crisis.February 14, 1932469165-9Increasing ruin of the Navajo tribe through government neglect following the storm disaster (2 copies).February 19, 1932469165-9"The apex of meanness" the Mescalero expenditures.February 20, 1932469165-9To the members of congress and to friends of the Indian everywhere, letter signed by Indian leaders charging deplorable conditions of Indians.February 26, 1932469165-9Highlights of the case for removing H.J. Hagerman from the government payroll.February 29, 1932469165-9Resolution calling for government aid for Indians in distress and for the saving of the Navajo sheep. House Committee Resolution.March 2, 1932469165-9Open letter concerning forthcoming statement to be introduced into the Congressional record.March 8, 1932469165-9Memorandum for the Press, Secretary Wilbur's response to Senator King's statement in the Senate.March 9, 1932469165-9Statement by John Collier in reply to a statement by Secretary Ray Lyman Wilbur of the Interior Department.469165-9Pueblo council challenges Wilbur to prove statements.March 10, 1932March 15, 1932469165-9Editorial. Albuquerque Journal. March 17, 1932, "Mr. Hagerman's salary."March 17, 1932469165-9Editorial. New Mexico State Tribune. March 18, 1932, "The Hagerman Ouster."March 18, 1932469165-9Editorial. Albuquerque Journal March 18, 1932, Secretary Wilbur's insulting letter to Isleta Pueblo Council.March 18, 1932469165-9"Indian Truth"? Controversy between the Indian Rights Association and the American Indian Defense Association.March 24, 1932469165-9Do you know? statistics on Indian Bureau expenditures.March 31, 1932469165-9Give and take between the Secretary of the Interior and the Indian tribes.April 6, 1932469165-9More light on the Yakima Water Rights Bill.April 7, 1932469165-9Cause of the delay of the Pueblo Bill.April 14, 1932469165-9Press release declaring Bill H.R. 10351 a menace to all Indian tribes.April 22, 1932469165-9Proposed repeal of espionage and marital law statutes affecting Indians.April 26, 1932469165-9A bill to gag and terrorize the employees of executive departments.May 6, 1932469165-9International Committee on the graphic display of the Native Races of America.May 17, 1932469165-9Needs in administration of Indian property.May 20, 1932469165-9Letter to Commissioner of Indian Affairs protesting the proposed alienation of thirteen reservoir sites and Mountain Lakes, property of the Flathead tribe of Montana.May 28, 1932469165-9Shall the Hagerman Agreement divesting the Walapai tribe of Arizona be confirmed.May 30, 1932469165-9The Walapai issue stated in a nutshell.June 8, 1932469165-9Why constructive Indian legislation is not being passed.June 2, 1932469165-10American Indian Defense Association Inc..October 28, 1932469165-10A statement about the Albuquerque Indian Boarding School under superintendent Reuben Perry who is now a candidate for assistant commissioner of Indian Affairs.January 20, 1933469165-11Immediate tasks and events in Indian defense.January 21, 1933469165-11The destruction of Indian tribal capital by the Indian Bureau in the year 1932.February 13, 1933469165-11Information bulletin: of interest to the Pueblos, the Navajos and the allotted Indians.February 16, 1933469165-11Wheeler-Howard appropriations total over six million dollars.February 1, 1935469165-12House Committee ignores demand for Collier's removal.February 7, 1935469165-12Important arts and crafts bill gets Senate Committee's approval.March 18, 1935469165-12The Federal Government cannot evade its obligation to help the public school districts meet the costs of educating Indian youth.March 26, 1935469165-12Senate Indian Committee opens public hearings on Oklahoma Welfare bill.April 8, 1935469165-12Senate Committee concludes hearings on Oklahoma Welfare Bill---Grady Lewis presents moving plea for five tribes---Joseph Briner is lone dissenter.April 11, 1935469165-12House Indian Committee opens public hearings on Thomas Rogers Bill-Creeks and Choctaws and Chickasaws submit endorsement.April 22, 1935469165-12Suggested amendments to the Thomas-Rogers Bill.April 23, 1935469165-12Issue of recovering federal jurisdiction over Five Civilized Tribes emerges as contested point at second hearing on Thomas-Rogers Bill.April 24, 1935469165-12State courts defended by Eufaula Guardian at third hearing on Thomas-Rogers Bill-Grady Lewis presents cases to support charges.April 25, 1935469165-12Clark Nicholas concludes testimony on Thomas-Rogers Bill-attacks argument of Grady Lewis.April 26, 1935469165-12A 'dirt farmer" views the Oklahoma Indian problem extracts from letters of a white neighbor of the Cherokees which abound in great common sense.May 15, 1935469165-12Some more common sense from an Oklahoma farmer this time anent the abolition of the Indian Bureau and the campaign against the Thomas-Rogers Bill.May 25, 1935469165-12The company is fighting the arts and crafts bill.May 28, 1935469165-12House Indian Committee bills Oklahoma Welfare Bill without discussion. Hopes now center on Senator Thomas to secure passage of Senate bill.May 29, 1935469165-12Bill to give tribes participating control over tribal funds is introduced.June 1, 1935469165-12 ======= Series I. The attack against the Carlos Apache ...Interior Department Bill.February 3, 1928469165-2No. 9-A The proof that Indian welfare effort succeeds and the justification for a new appeal for aid.February 20 1928469165-3To the press of New Mexico: position of the American Indian Defense Association on the middle Rio Conservancy Bill.February 25, 1928469165-3The Rio Grande Conservancy Bill (Pueblo) correction of a mis-statement and some important new facts.February 27, 1928469165-3No. 25. American Indian Defense Association, Inc. papersGuide469165-4Letter to Pueblo tribal officials.November 22, 1927469165-5Written statement to the "Oakland Forum".December 14, 1927469165-5Secretary Work confirms the "Irresponsible Propaganda" and light starts to break on Indian affairs.December 16, 1927469165-5Letter to the members Congress.December 20, 1927469165-5The hidden crisis in forestry situation.January 4, 1928469165-5Letter to Senator Smoot.January 19, 1928469165-5Letter to the officers of the New Mexico Pueblos.January 21, 1928469165-5Letter to members of U.S. Senate in regard to pending vote on Flathead power-site (2 copies).February 2, 1928469165-5The money stake of the Indians in the Flathead Power-site lease controversy.February 6, 1928469165-5"Recall this bill and protect Pueblo Indians"! (Senate Bill 700).February 8, 1928469165-5To the members of the Senate "Broken faith with the Indians and a trick upon Congress", the Pueblo Conservancy Bill (S.700).February 9, 1928469165-5Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy.February 11, 1928469165-5To Senators: the Pueblo outrage which jeopardizes all Indians.February 12, 1928469165-5Rio Grande Conservancy Bill sent back to committee.February 13, 1928469165-5Does the language divert the Flathead Indian revenues?February 14, 1928469165-5To the members of the House and Senate: the Cramton Pueblo Conservancy outrage.February 16, 1928469165-5To the officials of the Pueblos: the Conservancy Bill.February 17, 1928469165-5Statement by John Collier and A. A. Grorud, dealing with certain statements by representative Louis C. Cramton, made on the floor of the House, February 16, 1928.February 17, 1928469165-5To the members of congress: the acknowledged facts which condemn the pending Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy Bill.February 19, 1928469165-5To the several newspapers associations: Middle Rio Grande Pueblo Conservancy Bill.February 22, 1928469165-5Mary Austin declares amended Pueblo Conservancy Bill a menace to the Indians and to the Spanish-American white farmers of New MexicoFebruary 23, 1928469165-5A final plea to the Senate on behalf of the Pueblo Indians and of simple national honor.February 24, 1928469165-5"Has the Pueblo irrigated acreage been shrinking within historic times"?February 29, 1928469165-5Bulletin No. 1 of series "Conservancy and reclamation on Indian Lands"; "The nightmare of Indian Bureau reclamation and Conservancy,".January 19, 1928469165-6Fictions and mysteries of the Indian Bureau. standard for Indian boarding school children requires in the 1931 Interior Appropriation.March 22, 1930469165-7The record and position of the Indian Bureau, showing that the Indian children must rely Fall record.April 21, 1930469165-7Subsidizing the Anaconda Copper Company in Montana.April 22, 1930469165-7Defeat this Indian Bureau Bill establishing tyranny over the Indians!April 25, 1930469165-7A new espionage over Indians proposed by the new Indian commissioners.April 29, 1930469165-7Copy of the letter addressed by the Board of Directors of the American Indian Defense Association, Inc., to Secretary Ray Lyman Wilbur of the Department of the Interior.May 6, 1930469165-7Editorial page 310, The New Republic, May 7, 1930. A letter by the Executive Secretary to the President of the Association dealing with Secretary Wilbur's letter.May 7, 1930469165-7I. A letter by Dr. Haven Emerson to Secretary Wilbur.May 7, 1930469165-7II. A letter by the Board of Directors to Secretary Wilbur.May 9, 1930469165-7Cramton and the power trust steal a march on Congress.May 9, 1930469165-7The Flathead license outrage and the Interior Department's ironical defense.May 20, 1930469165-7The Flathead license subverts the federal water power act and destroys public regulation.May 31, 1930469165-7Letter to the officers of the New Mexico Pueblos concerning Bill H.R. 12615.June 3, 1930469165-7The betrayal of regulation through the Flathead power site license.June 4, 1930469165-7Declares Simms Bill a threat to Pueblo Indian life.June 8, 1930469165-7Letter from Secretary of the Interior Wilbur in regards to Flathead Power site.June 12, 1930469165-7The birthright of regulation sold, and the mess of pottage not secured in exchange by the unprecedented Flathead power license.June 18, 1930469165-7The Indian Bureau when defying parental wishes and forcing Indian children into boarding schools.Undated 1931469165-8Senator Frazier, Committee Chairman, moves to protect Pueblo Indians and white settlers of New Mexico (2 copies).January 24, 1931469165-8Representative passes out March 2nd. Thereafter, the Swing-Johnson Bill can certainly be enacted.January 28, 1931469165-8The case against Herbert J. Hagerman of the Indian Bureau as established to date.February 5, 1931469165-8Indian education after two years, the situation demands action, not delay through a $100,000 self-investigation by the delinquent Indian Bureau.February 25, 1931469165-8The Indian Bureau charged with deception or with being the victim of deception in the matter of Navajo land adjustments.February 25, 1931469165-8Dr. Hoffman insists: "All that can reasonably be done is being done to promote the of our Indians." Is it true? Hagerman from the government payroll.February 29, 1932469165-9Resolution calling for government aid for Indians in distress and for the saving of the Navajo sheep. 1932469165-9Why constructive Indian legislation is not being passed.June 2, 1932469165-10American Indian Defense Association Inc..October 28, 1932469165-10A statement about the Albuquerque Indian Boarding School under superintendent Reuben Perry who is now a candidate for assistant commissioner of Indian Affairs.January 20, 1933469165-11Immediate tasks and events in Indian defense.January 21, 1933469165-11The destruction of Indian tribal capital by the Indian Bureau in the year 1932.February 13, 1933469165-11Information bulletin: of interest to the Pueblos, the Navajos and the allotted Indians.February 16, 1933469165-11Wheeler-Howard appropriations total over six million dollars.February 1, 1935469165-12House Committee ignores demand for Collier's removal.February 7, 1935469165-12Important arts and crafts bill gets Senate Committee's approval.March 18, 1935469165-12The Federal Government cannot evade its obligation to help the public school districts meet the costs of educating Indian youth.March 26, 1935469165-12Senate Indian Committee opens public hearings on Oklahoma Welfare bill.April 8, 1935469165-12Senate Committee concludes hearings on Oklahoma Welfare Bill---Grady Lewis presents moving plea for five tribes---Joseph Briner is lone dissenter.April 11, 1935469165-12House Indian Committee opens public hearings on Thomas Rogers Bill-Creeks and Choctaws and Chickasaws submit endorsement.April 22, 1935469165-12Suggested amendments to the Thomas-Rogers Bill.April 23, 1935469165-12Issue of recovering federal jurisdiction over Five Civilized Tribes emerges as contested point at second hearing on Thomas-Rogers Bill.April 24, 1935469165-12State courts defended by Eufaula Guardian at third hearing on Thomas-Rogers Bill-Grady Lewis presents cases to support charges.April 25, 1935469165-12Clark Nicholas concludes testimony on Thomas-Rogers Bill-attacks argument of Grady Lewis.April 26, 1935469165-12A 'dirt farmer" views the Oklahoma Indian problem extracts from letters of a white neighbor of the Cherokees which abound in great common sense.May 15, 1935469165-12Some more common sense from an Oklahoma farmer this time anent the abolition of the Indian Bureau and the campaign against the Thomas-Rogers Bill.May 25, 1935469165-12The company is fighting the arts and crafts bill.May 28, 1935469165-12House Indian Committee bills Oklahoma Welfare Bill without discussion.
